title: Floating Bench for a Nook or Alcove
summary: A wall-mounted bench with no visible legs — a hidden 2x4 frame screwed into studs carries a 1x6 seat top that looks like it's floating.
description:
There's no leg framework here at all — the entire bench hangs off a 2x4 ladder frame screwed directly into wall studs, which is what gives it the floating look. That means the build lives or dies on stud location: find every stud in the mounting area before you cut a single board, because the frame has to land on them, not just the wall.

This plan is written around a representative 48"-wide, 16"-deep nook — the source build is genuinely build-to-fit for whatever alcove you have, so treat the dimensions here as a worked example and re-measure your own space before cutting. Anything over about 16" deep or 4 feet of unsupported span benefits from a center support board and heavier 2x4 (rather than 2x3) framing.

The front and side trim is optional. Skip it for a minimal floating-shelf look, or add it to hide the seam between the plywood frame and the wall and protect the wall from scuffing — either way it's cosmetic, not structural.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs, plus letting screws set before sitting on it

tools.note: Marking the mounting line — everything else registers off it.

materials.name: Pine, 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Hidden wall-mounted frame. 2x3 saves depth but use 2x4 if your span exceeds 4 ft.

materials.name: Pine, 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Seat top boards, joined edge to edge.

materials.name: Pine, 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Front trim, and optional side/back trim.

materials.name: 3" self-tapping wood screws
materials.note: Frame to studs — a star-bit head (SPAX, GRK, or similar) is worth it.

cutList.note: Depth minus 2-1/4" — sized for a 16" finished seat depth.
Screwed to studs along the wall.
Recommended for seat depths over 16" or spans over 4 ft.

steps.title: Mark the mounting line and find studs
steps.body: Mark a level line at 17-1/4" off the floor around the bench area — that's a standard seat height. Locate and mark every stud just above the line with a stud finder; the whole frame's strength depends on hitting real framing, not just drywall.

steps.title: Attach the side frame pieces
steps.body: Cut two 2x4 (or 2x3) pieces to your desired finished bench depth minus 2-1/4". Attach them to the side walls with 3" self-tapping screws into studs, top edge flush to your pencil line.

steps.title: Attach the back frame piece
steps.body: Measure and cut a 2x4 for the back wall and screw it into studs along your line. You can notch around electrical outlets if needed — just make sure every segment still lands on at least two studs.

steps.title: Add a center support for wider benches
steps.body: For seat depths greater than about 16", cut and add a center support running front to back, attached with pocket holes or 3" screws driven at an angle into the end boards. This keeps the middle of a wide seat from flexing under weight.

steps.title: Attach the front frame piece
steps.body: Measure and cut the front 2x4 to fit between the side pieces and attach it with two 3" screws at each end.

steps.title: Test the frame before adding the top
steps.body: Push down firmly on the center of the frame. If it flexes noticeably, add a center leg down to the floor under the middle support, or reinforce as part of the front trim — better to catch this now than after the top is on.

steps.title: Attach the seat top
steps.body: Join the 1x6 boards edge to edge before installing so the top goes on as one piece — any thick 1x material works here, including 1x12s or 3/4" plywood. Cut to fit, with an even overhang on the front if you're not adding front trim.

steps.title: Add trim
steps.body: Trim the front edge with a 1x4 board, glued and nailed. Optional side and back trim conceals the gap between the frame and the wall and protects the wall from scuffing — skip it for a cleaner, more minimal look.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand all edges, fill any visible fastener holes, and finish to match the room. Give screws and glue a day to fully set before loading the bench with weight.

images.alt: A wall-mounted bench with no visible legs, built into a wall nook
